records.ety: [OE. bla, blo, blew, blue, livid, black, fr. Icel.blār livid; akin to Dan. blaa blue, Sw. bl, D. blauw, OHG. blāo, G. blau; but influenced in form by F. bleu, from OHG. blāo.]

defs: Having the color of the clear sky, or a hue resembling it, whether lighter or darker; as, the deep, blue sea; as blue as a sapphire; blue violets.
Pale, without redness or glare, -- said of a flame; hence, of the color of burning brimstone, betokening the presence of ghosts or devils; as, the candle burns blue; the air was blue with oaths.
Low in spirits; melancholy; as, to feel blue.
Suited to produce low spirits; gloomy in prospect; as, thongs looked blue.
Severe or over strict in morals; gloom; as, blue and sour religionists; suiting one who is over strict in morals; inculcating an impracticable, severe, or gloomy mortality; as, blue laws.
Literary; -- applied to women; -- an abbreviation of bluestocking.
quotes: The ladies were very blue and well informed.
For his religion . . . 'T was Presbyterian, true blue.

defs: One of the seven colors into which the rays of light divide themselves, when refracted through a glass prism; the color of the clear sky, or a color resembling that, whether lighter or darker; a pigment having such color.
the sky; as, to fly off into the blue.
Low spirits; a fit of despondency; melancholy.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Blued (<?/); p. pr. & vb. n. Bluing.]

defs: To make blue; to dye of a blue color; to make blue by heating, as metals, etc.
